invariant_definition: *Bheda* is real difference or distinction, not just conceptual contrast. The term asks whether the world is finally articulated by irreducible distinctions between knower, known, means of knowledge, cause, effect, God, soul, and matter. It therefore stands at the center of Vedāntic metaphysics and scriptural interpretation. It also underwrites the grammar of devotion and inference, since worship, predication, and valid cognition all presuppose some standing distinction among their terms. Advaita treats *bheda* as empirically operative but ultimately sublatable under non-dual knowledge. Viśiṣṭādvaita affirms real distinctions within an organic whole ordered by Brahman. Tattva-vāda (the school often labeled Dvaita) radicalizes the point into *pañca-bheda*, five eternal distinctions, thereby defending a realist hierarchical pluralism rather than a crude two-substance dualism. Bhedābheda schools deny that difference can be eliminated, but refuse to let it exhaust reality. In practice *bheda* governs worship, ethics, agency, and causality: if real distinctions are only apparent, those domains must be re-described; if they are ultimate, they define the permanent structure of things.

per_school.definition: *Bheda* belongs to *vyavahāra* as the common structure of dream and waking appearances, but it is not *paramārtha*, since the parity of differentiations is itself what licenses their sublation in knowledge [[MK 2.5-6](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.Vaitathya-prakaraṇa.5-6)]. The manifold of distinctions is therefore a pedagogic and phenomenally operative presentation rather than an ultimate ontological articulation, and Brahman is determined precisely through the dissolution of the *bheda-prapañca* [[MK 3.15](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.15)] [[MK 3.18](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.18)] [[MBs 1.2](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.2)] [[MBs 1.3](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.3)].

*Bheda* is real because Bhagavān, taught under the many Vedāntic designators, remains *svetara-samasta-vastu-vilakṣaṇa* even while standing as the inner self of every *cit* and *acit* through co-referential predication [[VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. Karmically produced differentiations such as godhood and humanity do not constitute the self's *svarūpa*; when they are removed, the self remains a distinct *jñāna-svarūpa*, and cognition cannot be reduced to a bodily property [[VS §5](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5)] [[AS 6](cite://yamuna/atma-siddhi/6)].

*Bheda* is *anādi* and *satya*, articulated as the *bhedapañcaka* that structures the whole *prapañca* and cannot be dismissed without collapsing the ontic order itself [[Anuvy. 1.4.111-112](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.4.111-112)]. Because valid cognition is already difference-grasping, the distinction between *jīva* and Īśvara is fixed by both *pramāṇa* and *śruti*, and later Mādhva exegesis explicitly anchors this in *bhedavyapadeśa* and *suṣuptyutkrāntyor bhedena* [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)] [[TD 1.1](cite://raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1)] [[TD 1.3](cite://raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.3)].

*Bheda* is the real articulation of Brahman into *nāma-rūpa*, so manifestation is not a merely concessive appearance but an actual differentiation of what was previously unarticulated [[BṛU 1.4.7](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.7)]. The same Brahman is nonetheless taught through identity-statements and through the two-form presentation of formed and formless, so the manifold and the unitary are retained together within a single scriptural ontology [[BṛU 1.4.10](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.10)] [[BṛU 2.3.1, 2.3.6](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/2.3)].

*Bheda* between Bhagavān and the *jīva*-aggregate must be retained even while the *abheda-śāstras* are honored, because the two are coordinated only through Bhagavān's *svābhāvika acintya-śakti*, which supports both difference and non-difference without contradiction [[PS §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Once the Lord is fixed as the locus of *māyā* and the *jīva* as *māyā-mohita*, this standing difference grounds worship itself as the proper *abhidheya* [[PS §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)].

*Bheda* is *svābhāvika*: the *jīva* is atomic, dependent on Hari, and distinct in each body, yet remains an enduring knower rather than a severed or self-subsistent substance [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)]. The school's account of the soul accordingly preserves a plurality of bound, liberated, and devotional states, together with further internal differentiations, without relinquishing the *jīva*'s abiding relation to Bhagavān [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)].

*Bheda* is not an extrinsic rupture outside Brahman but the Veda's own manifold designation of the *jīva* as a real *aṃśa* of Brahman [[AṇuBh 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)]. Because this *aṃśatva* is scripturally known rather than modeled on an ordinary part-whole division, the soul's distinctness is preserved within Brahman's own reality rather than assigned to a merely adventitious adjunct [[AṇuBh 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)].

For Sāṃkhya, *bheda* is first the differentiated unfolding of *guṇa-pariṇāma*: plurality in the cognitive apparatus is explained through distinct transformations of the *guṇas* together with the difference of the objects grasped [[SK 27](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/27)] [[SS 2.27](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/2.27)]. Bondage persists through *aviveka*, the failure to discriminate *pradhāna* from what is not it, so release requires a completed *viveka* rather than the erasure of plurality into an undifferentiated absolute [[SS 1.57](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.57)].

In Yoga, the functional analogue of *bheda* is *sattva-puruṣānyatā-pratyaya*: *vivekakhyāti* is explicitly defined as the cognition that *sattva* and *puruṣa* are other than one another, and its unbroken stream is the direct means of cessation [[YBh 2.26](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.26)]. At the summit of practice, buddhisattva is reduced to the bare office of manifesting the puruṣa's otherness, and kaivalya follows when the seer is no longer re-entangled with the guṇic presentation that had seemed to share its status [[YBh 3.49](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.49)] [[YBh 3.55](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.55)].

In Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, the school's own equivalents for *bheda* are *pṛthaktva* and *viśeṣa*: separateness is a real guṇa, while *viśeṣa* names the ultimate differentiators that inhere in eternal substances [[VS 7.2.1](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/7.2.1)] [[AnTS 78](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/78)]. Difference is therefore objective rather than merely conceptual, since the categorial order requires both the commonality of *sāmānya* and the non-collapse secured by *viśeṣa* beyond ordinary generic determination [[VS 1.2.3-6](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.2.3-6)] [[AnTS 78](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/78)].

In Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, *bheda* is principally *karmabheda*: the differentiated identity of injunctions and performances by which one determines whether a rite is repeated, transferred, or held distinct from another [[MS 2.2.19](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.2.19)] [[MS 2.4.8](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.4.8)]. A difference in wording, name, form, *dharmaviśeṣa*, recurrence, or allied markers is sufficient to fix distinct ritual obligations, because subsidiary dravya- and guṇa-relations cannot erase the identity of the act to which they belong [[MS 2.2.19](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.2.19)] [[MS 2.4.8](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.4.8)].

*Bheda* first appears as the disciplined discrimination between the two truths, since without grasping that *vibhāga* one does not understand the Buddha's *gambhīra tattva* [[MMK 24.9](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.9)]. Yet no ultimately established distinguishing feature separates *saṃsāra* from *nirvāṇa*, so difference cannot be reified into *svabhāva* [[MMK 25.19](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.19)].

*Bheda* and *abheda* are both inadequate if taken as final ontological predications, since the perfected nature is said to be neither different from nor non-different from the dependent nature [[Triṃś 22](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/22)]. The relation is therefore speakable only in a controlled, pedagogic way internal to *paratantratā*, not as a pair of self-standing entities set over against one another [[Triṃś 22](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/22)].

In Pratyabhijñā, *bheda* is an *ābhāsa* of one consciousness under the power of *māyā*: the very cit of the Lord becomes a domain of distinct objects, cognitions, and determinations without ceasing to be itself [[ĪPK 1.5.18](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.18)]. The distinction between bondage and liberation turns on how this differentiated field is taken: the bound subject treats the common object-world as radically other, while the liberated knower repossesses it in *svātmābheda* as the manifestation of Maheśvara [[ĪPK 4.13](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4.13)].

In Jainism, *bheda* is a real operation within *pudgala*: matter is characterized by divisibility, and atoms and aggregates arise through division and aggregation [[TS 5.24-28](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.24-28)]. Because the real is defined by origination, cessation, and persistence together, division does not annihilate substance; it marks modal transformation within enduring *dravya* [[TS 5.29-30](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.29-30)].

translator_note: The lazy gloss is simply 'difference.' That can work, but only if one hears 'real distinction' rather than a weak conceptual contrast. Another common mistake is to turn Tattva-vāda's use of *bheda* into 'dualism' in a Cartesian sense. Madhva's view is not a flat two-term split but a graded realist plurality with permanent distinctions across many orders of being.
